criterion_breakdown.label: Browser product and design-system productivity
criterion_breakdown.note: Best fit for the route-heavy browser product, semantic selector model, and design-system iteration loop.

criterion_breakdown.label: Deterministic serialization and hashing
criterion_breakdown.note: Needs a deliberately centralized canonical serialization and hash library, but can share the same rules across browser, backend, and Playwright fixtures.

criterion_breakdown.label: Backend API, streaming, and concurrency fit
criterion_breakdown.note: A strong fit for northbound HTTP APIs, SSE-style streams, and I/O-bound authority integration, provided CPU-heavy deterministic math stays disciplined.

criterion_breakdown.label: Exact-decimal and money-safe computation support
criterion_breakdown.note: Requires strict domain-level decimal abstractions and explicit prohibition of JS `number` for money-bearing paths, but remains workable through canonical decimal strings and fixed-scale libraries.

criterion_breakdown.label: Contract and schema ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Strong JSON Schema tooling and shared type generation make the browser, backend, and tests align naturally.

criterion_breakdown.label: Security ecosystem maturity
criterion_breakdown.note: Mature enough for OIDC, secret management, supply-chain controls, and OpenTelemetry instrumentation, though it depends on disciplined dependency and runtime governance.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shared type and contract reuse across surfaces
criterion_breakdown.note: Best cross-surface reuse story because browser, backend, and Playwright can consume one generated TypeScript model layer.

criterion_breakdown.label: macOS-native coexistence strategy
criterion_breakdown.note: Pairs cleanly with a server-authoritative Swift client because TypeScript can own web/backend surfaces without trying to replace native UX.

criterion_breakdown.label: Playwright and frontend automation ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Matches the current Playwright footprint and keeps browser contracts, fixtures, and tests in one ecosystem.

criterion_breakdown.label: Observability ecosystem fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Strong OpenTelemetry support and good compatibility with the current Node/Playwright observability layer.

criterion_breakdown.label: Hiring, maintainability, and long-term operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Keeps the product core mostly in one runtime while still honoring the already-justified Python and Swift edges.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration complexity from current repo evidence
criterion_breakdown.note: Aligns directly with the current Node/Playwright footprint and preserves the Python tooling estate instead of displacing it.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance profile under expected workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Good enough for the expected I/O-heavy product surface, with room to isolate hot compute or stream fan-out paths later if profiling demands it.

alternatives.label: TypeScript/Node-centered product stack with Swift native and retained Python tooling
risks: Exact-decimal discipline must be aggressively enforced because the default JS number model is unsafe for money-bearing logic.
Deterministic serialization and hashing must live in one canonical library rather than being recreated in each service or client.
strengths: Best overall fit for the browser-first product surface and Playwright-first validation model.
Strongest shared-contract reuse story between browser UI, backend APIs, and automated tests.
Preserves the existing Python and Swift evidence without making either pretend to be the product-core runtime.
alternatives.summary: Use TypeScript on the current active Node LTS line for browser product surfaces, backend command/stream services, and shared runtime contract packages. Retain Python for corpus validators and analysis tooling, and retain Swift/SwiftUI plus targeted AppKit for the macOS client.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser product and design-system productivity
criterion_breakdown.note: The browser remains TypeScript, but the primary stack still spans two product runtimes instead of one.

criterion_breakdown.label: Deterministic serialization and hashing
criterion_breakdown.note: The JVM offers a very strong deterministic and schema-disciplined backend foundation when canonical serialization is centralized.

criterion_breakdown.label: Backend API, streaming, and concurrency fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Excellent fit for high-concurrency APIs, typed streaming, and exact backend domain models.

criterion_breakdown.label: Exact-decimal and money-safe computation support
criterion_breakdown.note: BigDecimal-backed exact-decimal handling is strong and credible for money-safe logic.

criterion_breakdown.label: Contract and schema ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Good schema ergonomics, but the browser/backend type seam still requires a stronger generation strategy than the TS-centered option.

criterion_breakdown.label: Security ecosystem maturity
criterion_breakdown.note: Strong ecosystem for OIDC, supply-chain controls, and service hardening.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shared type and contract reuse across surfaces
criterion_breakdown.note: Better than Python for strongly typed backend models, but still weaker than TS end-to-end reuse for browser, backend, and tests.

criterion_breakdown.label: macOS-native coexistence strategy
criterion_breakdown.note: Still compatible with a Swift native client because the native seam remains contract-based.

criterion_breakdown.label: Playwright and frontend automation ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Playwright remains strong on the frontend side, with the same caveat about cross-runtime contract drift.

criterion_breakdown.label: Observability ecosystem fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Excellent OpenTelemetry and JVM observability support.

criterion_breakdown.label: Hiring, maintainability, and long-term operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Operationally strong, but adds a new core runtime that the repo does not currently evidence.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration complexity from current repo evidence
criterion_breakdown.note: Requires introducing a completely new backend runtime while still retaining both TypeScript and Swift, increasing initial setup cost substantially.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance profile under expected workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Best raw backend headroom of the three alternatives, though that advantage is not yet the dominant phase-00 concern.

alternatives.label: Kotlin/JVM backend with TypeScript web and Swift native
risks: Adds a third serious implementation runtime before the repo has any JVM evidence.
Shared contract reuse with the browser layer is materially weaker than the TypeScript-centered option.
strengths: Strongest backend-only story for exact-decimal rigor, concurrency, and raw performance headroom.
Very credible for long-lived service operability once fully adopted.
alternatives.summary: Use Kotlin/JVM for the backend and deterministic compute core, TypeScript for browser surfaces and Playwright, and Swift for the macOS client.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser product and design-system productivity
criterion_breakdown.note: Browser productivity remains strong on the frontend, but the primary product stack still splits between frontend and backend concerns.

criterion_breakdown.label: Deterministic serialization and hashing
criterion_breakdown.note: Python can express deterministic serialization and replay loaders clearly, especially alongside the existing validator estate.

criterion_breakdown.label: Backend API, streaming, and concurrency fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Works well for typed APIs and orchestration, though stream fan-out and high-concurrency event handling often need more deliberate engineering choices.

criterion_breakdown.label: Exact-decimal and money-safe computation support
criterion_breakdown.note: Python's decimal support is excellent for the exact-decimal contract.

criterion_breakdown.label: Contract and schema ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Schema handling is solid, but the web/backend type boundary remains more fragmented than a TS-centered product core.

criterion_breakdown.label: Security ecosystem maturity
criterion_breakdown.note: Security tooling is mature, especially around validators and data processing, but shared runtime parity with the browser layer is weaker.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shared type and contract reuse across surfaces
criterion_breakdown.note: The browser stack and backend stack stay separated by codegen and schema wrappers more often, increasing semantic-drift pressure.

criterion_breakdown.label: macOS-native coexistence strategy
criterion_breakdown.note: Still pairs fine with Swift because the native client is separate from backend language choice.

criterion_breakdown.label: Playwright and frontend automation ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Playwright remains strong because the web layer stays TypeScript, but contract drift risk across the backend seam increases.

criterion_breakdown.label: Observability ecosystem fit
criterion_breakdown.note: OpenTelemetry support is viable, though cross-language correlation and instrumentation discipline become more important.

criterion_breakdown.label: Hiring, maintainability, and long-term operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Python is widely operable, but the product core becomes more polyglot sooner than necessary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration complexity from current repo evidence
criterion_breakdown.note: Preserves the current Python estate well, but does not capitalize as directly on the existing Node/Playwright browser and prototype footprint.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance profile under expected workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Adequate for expected workloads, but lower raw concurrency headroom than a well-tuned Node or JVM option for event-heavy product APIs.

alternatives.label: Python-centered backend with TypeScript web and Swift native
risks: Shared contract consumption across browser, backend, and tests becomes more fragmented.
The product core splits earlier into Python plus TypeScript, which weakens the one-primary-stack goal of ADR-001.
strengths: Strongest exact-decimal and validator continuity story with the current repo evidence.
Low conceptual drift between offline analysis tooling and backend implementation.
alternatives.summary: Use Python for the backend and deterministic compute core, TypeScript for browser surfaces and Playwright, and Swift for the native macOS client.

criteria.label: Deterministic serialization and hashing
criteria.rationale: Replay, manifest sealing, and attestation depend on byte-stable serialization and hash equality across runtime boundaries.

criteria.label: Exact-decimal and money-safe computation support
criteria.rationale: The compute and parity contracts explicitly forbid binary floating-point for money-bearing values and require canonical decimal-string persistence.

criteria.label: Contract and schema ergonomics
criteria.rationale: The corpus is schema-heavy, closed by default, and expects route-visible artifacts, stream payloads, and state objects to stay machine-checkable across services and clients.

criteria.label: Browser product and design-system productivity
criteria.rationale: The product surface is route-heavy, design-token-rich, and selector-governed across portal, collaboration, and governance shells.

criteria.label: Playwright and frontend automation ergonomics
criteria.rationale: Browser automation is a first-class delivery and validation requirement, not a testing afterthought.

criteria.label: Backend API, streaming, and concurrency fit
criteria.rationale: The backend must support typed command receipts, reconnect-safe streams, idempotent retries, and authority callbacks without compromising deterministic semantics.

criteria.label: Security ecosystem maturity
criteria.rationale: The runtime must credibly support short-lived sessions, secret isolation, supply-chain integrity, and fail-closed authority handling.

criteria.label: Observability ecosystem fit
criteria.rationale: The chosen runtime should work naturally with OpenTelemetry-style traces, metrics, logs, and correlation-aware audit overlays.

criteria.label: macOS-native coexistence strategy
criteria.rationale: The browser and backend runtime choice cannot erase the first-class Swift macOS client or force it into a browser wrapper posture.

criteria.label: Shared type and contract reuse across surfaces
criteria.rationale: The same contract set must drive browser routes, backend envelopes, automation fixtures, Python validators, and Swift native models without semantic drift.

criteria.label: Hiring, maintainability, and long-term operability
criteria.rationale: The primary stack should minimize gratuitous language fragmentation while keeping the supporting Python and Swift estates credible and maintainable.

criteria.label: Migration complexity from current repo evidence
criteria.rationale: Current repo evidence already shows Python analysis tooling and Node/Playwright browser automation, so the first product stack should preserve that leverage rather than restart from zero in every lane.

criteria.label: Performance profile under expected workloads
criteria.rationale: The product needs credible streaming, validation, and compute throughput, but there is not yet enough live workload evidence to let raw throughput dominate the phase-00 stack choice.

scoring_scale.interpretation: 5 = best fit against the corpus constraint, 1 = weak fit or high risk

winner.label: TypeScript/Node-centered product stack with Swift native and retained Python tooling
